title: Infantino at CNBC Forum: Iran Is 'Coming For Sure' to 2026 World Cup
detail: FIFA President Gianni Infantino made his most emphatic statement yet on Iran's World Cup participation, telling CNBC's 'Invest in America' forum that Iran is 'coming for sure.' Infantino referenced his personal attendance at Iran's 5-0 victory over Costa Rica in Antalya and said the team's quality convinced him of their commitment. The statement came 12 days before the FIFA Congress in Vancouver on April 30 — the formal decision point — and strengthened FIFA's position against any relocation or exclusion of Iran's Group G matches in Los Angeles and Seattle. Iran's government has not issued a revised public statement since the April 8 ceasefire.

media.caption: FIFA President Infantino confirms Iran participation at CNBC Invest in America Forum

title: SoFi Stadium Begins World Cup Pitch Construction in Los Angeles
detail: SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California officially launched construction and modification of its playing surface to meet FIFA's international soccer specifications for the 2026 World Cup. The $5.5 billion venue — opened in 2020 as the NFL's most expensive stadium — must install a FIFA-standard natural grass pitch over its artificial turf configuration. SoFi Stadium hosts Iran's Group G opener vs New Zealand on June 15 and potentially additional knockout-stage matches. The pitch work is part of the broader FIFA venue inspection timeline with the tournament 54 days away.

media.caption: SoFi Stadium workers begin laying the FIFA-standard grass pitch for the 2026 World Cup

title: New Jersey Governor Slams FIFA's '$0 Contribution' to World Cup Transit Costs
detail: New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill publicly condemned FIFA for providing zero funding toward transit infrastructure upgrades needed to handle World Cup crowds at MetLife Stadium, the site of the July 19 Final. Sherrill described FIFA's contribution as '$0' while NJ Transit has been forced to plan expensive rail service expansions — with train fares jumping from $13 to over $100 for match days — costs that will ultimately be borne by fans. The statement added the governor of New Jersey's voice to the growing chorus of host-city officials, fan groups, and elected officials criticizing FIFA's financial model which promises over $652M in prize money to teams but offers no direct infrastructure support to host cities.

media.caption: NJ Governor Sherrill criticizes FIFA's zero financial contribution to MetLife Stadium transit upgrades
